122 - We Already Have A Company for that Objection - YouTube.mp4
So today I'm going to go over about a 20 to 25 minute training on how do you disrupt? Like if you're talking to a prospect. And they're already with the company. They're already... with a vendor. Let's say, if you sell B2B or B2C, it doesn't matter. They already have a company or vendor they're already using. And let's say they like who they're already using. How do you still penetrate that account? How do you still make that sale? If they're already with another company and they like the other company, how do you get them to unlike the other company enough to be open? To going through what you have to offer them that could be better. How do you do that? First of all, go down to the bottom of this video, hit the subscribe button.

So how do you disrupt a relationship? relationship with a company or even a prospect. So if you're selling B2B, how do you disrupt a relationship with a company you're trying to penetrate? Or if you sell B2C, how do you penetrate a... person, an individual who likes the company they already are working with. I know you guys run into prospects every day that could use your services or products, but they're so happy with who they already have, even if they don't know any better, that they could get something better from you, and they emotionally shut down by the way you approach them.
So let's stop that from happening. The trust they have with that company that would cause them to be open to your solution and working with you so we're gonna give you— I'm gonna give you about five to six NEPQ questions that are designed to specifically do this. If it's B2B, more than likely you're going to say 'vendor.' You can still say 'company' as well, but you can use those terms interchangeably. Can I ask in what ways your company that you're with could possibly do better for you than they are now? Okay? It's a simple, neutral question. Now...
I'm going to give you this really important question. I'm actually going to give you about nine or ten questions. I'm feeling really nice. The question I'm about to give you helps you ease into letting your prospect know that they're making the wrong decision without them getting offended with you and without them feeling you were just trying to sell them. So let's say you go through your entire sales process, whether you sell B2B or you sell B2C, and at the end of that, they just say, 'Well, I think we're just going to stay with who we already have.' Well, what do you do then? Okay? Here's the question you want to ask. It works every freaking time. Make sure you write this down. Of any questions I just gave you today, this is probably the most important, okay?
Now, so if they said, 'we're just going to stick with who we already have at the end of your presentation or your sales process,' you're going to ask this question. No, you cannot communicate with me without me getting upset at you. They're going to say, 'No, yeah, sure, you're not offending me.' Like, what do you have in mind? That's exactly what they're going to say every time. If you say it with the right tonality.
Thanks, Dave Prosser, I know you. John, how would I be able to communicate with you? That you might be making the wrong decision. Without you getting upset with me. Oh, don't worry, I'm not going to get upset with you. What do you have in mind? It will automatically trigger them to say, 'Oh, I'm not going to get upset with you.' What do you have in mind? Without you getting upset with me. Oh, don't worry, Jeremy, I wouldn't get upset with you. What do you have in mind? Instantly disarms the prospect. See how that works? All right, let's keep going here, all right? Now, I'm going to give you a couple more, and I'm going to give you about three or four different industry-specific examples on how to use some of these questions I just went over so you can see how this works.
Also, use something that's called the two truths question. This is one we teach a lot to our clients in our virtual training courses and our group trainings as well that we do every week for our clients. Okay, two truths question. No one likes 100% of what they have or they're doing. There's always something they would change if they could. Think about the car you just bought two years ago. Let's say you bought a free... $350,000 Lamborghini, I guarantee you two years later, there's going to be something you don't like about that car, even though you fell in love with it the very first day. 100%. There'll always be something you don't like or you're going to change. So this two-choice question can be both used in B2C and B2B sales.
So here's the generic version. So Sally, I mean, to me, it sounds like things are going fairly well with who you're already using now. I mean, is there anything you would change about who you're using and the results you're getting if you... If you could. Notice my tonality. So, John, to me, I mean, it sounds like things are going fairly well with who you're already with. I mean, is there anything you would change about what you're using with them or the results you're getting? If you could, that question is almost like, well, why the hell am I even on this call with you? Everything sounds perfect. And they're going to say, 'We like them,' but, and then there's always something they're going to want to change.
Now, let me give you a few different industry examples. I'm going to give you a couple B2C examples, industries. I'm going to give you one or two B2B examples so you can see how this... truth question framework would actually work, okay? So let's say, if you sold life insurance. So Amy, I mean, to me, it sounds like things are going fairly well with the company you're already with. I mean, is there anything you would change about the coverage, the financial protection that you have with them for your family if you... If you could. So, John, to me it sounds like things are fairly going well with the policy you already have. Is there anything you would change about your financial protection you have for the kids if you could?
It's like, why the hell am I even on this call? Everything sounds perfect, okay? Now, I only need to ask the two truths question if they tell me they love everything about who they're already with. If they don't tell me... they love everything about who they're with, I would never need to ask the two truths question because it wouldn't be relevant in that conversation, okay? Now, let me give you another example. Well, and they might say, well, we like it, but we would maybe look at, okay, but having that, why so important to you now though? Why not just push it down the road like a lot of people would? Well, the reason why we need to look at it now is because of X and Y and Z.
Okay, here's a B2B example. Let's say if you sold business consulting to business owners that helps them get better systems and processes to scale their companies, train a lot of people in that industry. Well, I mean, don't get me wrong, we like them, but if we could...
Okay, but getting those type of results, why so important to you? Now though, like, why not push it down the road like a lot of unsuccessful business owners would? Well, the reason why we can't push it down the road is because of X and Y and Z and now they just told themselves, 'Why can't push it down the road?' Here's another example. I mean, to me... It sounds like things are going fairly well with the implants you've already been using the past decade.
